data.title: Spotting Clouds in a Carefree Summer Market — Streetwise — WSJ
data.description:
By James Mackintosh

On a sunny summer's day, you might think of going to the beach. Or you might list the things that ought to be worrying investors, and mostly aren't. Your scribe chose the latter, as regular readers might expect.

The list of what is actually giving investors pause is remarkably short, itself a reason for concern. If stocks climb a wall of worry, they may be approaching the top — and the nasty slide down the slope of hope.

What should be concerning them divides into three: the economy, stock valuations and politics.

The economic outlook boils down to one neat question: Will the president's hike in tariffs to their highest since the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act in the 1930s matter? Investors have swung in their answer. They initially treated the "Liberation Day" tariffs as a disaster for growth and inflation, before concluding that the tariffs at the mostly lower rates eventually imposed don't much matter — in large part because the feared effects have barely shown up in the data.

Futures traders now think a Federal Reserve rate cut is a slam dunk next month, something usually good for stocks when it isn't due to weaker growth.

Yet take off the rose-tinted sunglasses and there's a risk that the markets are ignoring. Far from coming down, core inflation excluding volatile energy and food rose last month to the highest level since President Trump's inauguration. Other measures that try to tease out the trend also point to an inflation issue, albeit nothing like the spike after the Biden-administration Covid-19 stimulus.

Take the Atlanta Fed's gauge of hard-to-change, or "sticky," prices and the Cleveland Fed's measure of the median item in the basket of items used to measure inflation. As of last month, both metrics were rising at an annual rate of well above 3%. Wholesale prices jumped last month by the most in three years, suggesting that tariff effects are working their way through the supply chain and will eventually push up inflation. Maybe tariffs are just taking longer than expected to hit.

The jobs market is also concerning. Private-sector job creation has stalled, with the report showing this infuriating the president so much he fired the head of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, which also produces the inflation figures.

A move toward mild stagflation, or weaker growth and higher inflation, is far from certain. But the data suggest it is absolutely a risk, and it is even visible in Wall Street earnings forecasts, if you squint. It just doesn't show up in stock prices.

The consensus for S&P 500 earnings in 12 months is 6% higher than at the start of the year, which — if believed — would justify rising stocks. But earnings upgrades have been heavily skewed toward three successful sectors: tech, communication services (where Meta and Alphabet sit) and financials. Four out of 11 sectors — energy, materials, healthcare and consumer staples — have been downgraded this year, and another two are barely changed.

One plausible explanation: The artificial-intelligence boom and data-center construction is hiding pain in other areas. That's not a great foundation for record-high stocks.

Stress-screen factor: 30.

Valuations show no worry at all. Stocks' price to earnings ratio, using forecast profits for the next 12 months, last month hit 22.5. That is the highest in data since 1985 apart from during the dot-com bubble of 1999-2000 and the SPAC/cannabis/clean-tech bubble of 2020-21.

This valuation gauge is down a bit this month because analysts have upgraded their profit forecasts by the most in more than a year, but is still extraordinarily high. Lofty valuations also show up in gauges such as the Shiller P/E, and ratios of prices to free cash flow and to book value.

The good news is that high valuations are no barrier to short-term performance. But they show marked optimism about future profits, raising the risk of disappointment and low long-term returns, especially if the economy or AI fail to fulfill hopes.

The bad news is that the high valuations are combined with profit margins expected to hit a new high for listed companies in data going back to 2002. Investors expect the best of all possible worlds.

Stress-screen factor: 50.

Politics suggests we may not get the best of all possible worlds. Sure, tariffs turned out less bad than feared. Even if the most extreme claims of Democrats that democracy is dying turned out to be right, investors have long been able to make good returns from stocks in autocracies, so long as the autocrat doesn't fall foul of more-powerful countries.

But the president's firing of the BLS head and attacks on the Federal Reserve mean the U.S. is less likely to get decent data on its most important statistics or nonpolitical interest-rate setting.

The risk shows up in long-run inflation expectations, measured as the gap between standard Treasury yields and those on Treasury inflation-protected securities for the five years starting in five years' time. These jump every time Trump wades in — but not that severely. Investors are alert to the danger, but still expect Trump to leave a functioning system in place.

The danger to investors is that the Fed loses its independence. If that happened, even if Trump set rates exactly right, Treasurys would be likely to attract a political risk premium, with knock-on effects on financing costs across the economy.

Stress-screen factor: Unknown, between 0 and Argentina.

All these are merely risks, and everything could work out fine. Maybe I'm the guy on the beach sitting in the shade complaining about the heat. But when obvious dangers are ignored, it isn't usually a good time to invest for the long run.

Write to James Mackintosh at james.mackintosh@wsj.com

data.title: Six Flags Is Having a Seriously Scary Summer — WSJ
data.description:
By Jacob Passy

To understand the challenges facing Six Flags Entertainment, look no further than Siren's Curse.

The new roller coaster opened in late June to rave reviews at Cedar Point, its theme park on the shore of Lake Erie halfway between Cleveland and Toledo, Ohio. The problem: Siren's Curse has broken down so many times that it's becoming a punchline. Maybe, the joke goes, the ride is actually cursed.

On July 26, it went out of service for at least the fifth time since its debut, with riders stranded at the top of the hill at the start of the ride. Videos of the incident went viral, showing riders exiting the ride in a less-than-thrilling fashion, descending a narrow staircase after being stuck over 100 feet in the air.

Six Flags has endured a nightmare of a summer. A laundry list of problems impeded the critical first half of the summer when visitors are most inclined to buy season passes to the company's 42 theme and water parks across North America.

Severe thunderstorms and excessive heat have dampened attendance and hampered operations, prompting some locations to shut down completely for several days. New rides that the company has been promising its thrill-seeking fans have been delayed. Rivals are moving in on its turf. Some longtime aficionados say the quality of the parks is suffering. Economic uncertainty is causing some people to rethink their leisure spending.

In the latest quarter, which ended June 29, it posted a 9% drop in attendance from the previous year. And the company just reported its third consecutive quarterly loss.

"Our financial results through the first six months of the year reflect a significant decline in attendance," Chief Executive Richard Zimmerman said during an earnings call with analysts this month. "Our job is to manage through short-term disruptions and focus on the things we can control."

That won't be Zimmerman's job for long. The same day as Six Flags announced its latest loss, it revealed Zimmerman would be stepping down by year's end. The company declined to make Zimmerman available for an interview.

Changing fortunes

The Six Flags that's struggling this summer is the product of a roughly $2 billion all-stock merger between Six Flags and a company called Cedar Fair that was announced in late 2023. Cedar Fair owned famed theme parks such as Cedar Point, Carowinds in North Carolina and Knott's Berry Farm in California.

The deal was a long time in the making. Six Flags had made a bid to acquire Cedar Fair back in 2019, which the latter company rejected. Cedar Fair also later turned down a takeover offer from SeaWorld.

The Covid-19 pandemic changed the fortunes of both chains, leaving them largely bereft of a year's worth of business and saddled with billions of dollars in debt. Attendance at both companies' theme parks has yet to regain their 2019 levels.

While the combined company retained the Six Flags name, analysts and theme-park veterans were heartened when the deal was announced to see that its leadership would come from Cedar Fair. That included Zimmerman, who had risen through Cedar Fair's ranks since he was named general manager of its Kings Dominion park in Virginia in 1998.

Six Flags had the more recognizable national brand, an impressive slate of thrill rides and access to popular intellectual properties from Warner Bros., such as Batman, Superman and Bugs Bunny. Operationally, though, the company was viewed as more inconsistent than rival Cedar Fair, said Scott Swenson, a theme-park consultant.

The companies said that the merger would help them cut costs, projecting $120 million in savings in the first two years alone. They also promised that, by combining, they could experiment with new pricing strategies, particularly in the handful of markets where both had parks — including Southern California, the Mid-Atlantic and the Great Lakes region. Zimmerman's challenge would be to steer the integration while appeasing investors and park visitors alike.

The company shed hundreds of employees as it consolidated corporate offices and combined teams, including those for marketing and IT. Six Flags also adopted a regional model for day-to-day operations at its parks to streamline decision-making across the chain.

Never-ending arms race

A visit to the local Six Flags theme park used to be a hallmark of an all-American summer. But Americans have lots of ways to spend their entertainment dollars these days.

Parks aimed at families with young children are popping up across the country, cashing in on franchises such as Lego, Mattel and Peppa Pig. These parks, which are generally smaller in size, are competing for the lower end of the age range that once might have considered Six Flags as its local, go-to theme park.

More of these types of parks are on the way. Comcast-owned Universal plans to open a family-oriented regional theme park prototype in Texas next year just a stone's throw from the original Six Flags. Called the Universal Kids Resort, it will feature areas based on popular films and TV shows like Minions, Shrek and SpongeBob SquarePants.

Meanwhile, for Americans willing to spend big on a theme-park visit, Universal and Walt Disney have been upping their game. Universal recently opened its third theme park in Orlando, spending about $7 billion on the most high-tech theme park ever built. Disney has committed to investing $60 billion into its existing theme parks and cruise line.

Even the rise of companies like Topgolf, which gamified the driving range, and upscale bowling alley Bowlero are siphoning off money that might once have been spent at Six Flags, analysts said.

"We compete for people's leisure time," Zimmerman said at the time of the merger. "They can decide to come to a theme park or they can do a lot of different things with their weekend time."

Few of those things are cheap, and Americans these days are growing more cautious about how they spend their money. Single-day admission to Six Flags Over Texas, near Dallas, would cost a family of four around $180 on a Saturday in late August. That's slightly more than the $120 it would cost to visit the nearby Peppa Pig Theme Park the same day, but far less than a day at Disney's Magic Kingdom theme park in Florida ($586 or more, depending on their ages).

One way that theme parks can create buzz and lure visitors is by building bigger, taller, wilder rides. It's a never-ending arms race between theme-park operators.

Six Flags has boasted some successes. At Canada's Wonderland in Ontario, the company opened the new AlpenFury multi-launch coaster in mid-July. The 1,000-meter-long ride drove a 20% increase in attendance at the park compared with the past year, the company said.

Siren's Curse at Cedar Point is a marquee attraction as one of the first "tilt" coasters in North America. The ride mechanically tilts riders downward at a 90-degree angle from the top of the 160-foot lift hill, before dropping them. The breakdowns have occurred when a safety mechanism has stopped the coaster's train at the top of that hill.

A spokesman for Six Flags said the ride has exhibited "very good overall reliability" and has operated during 92% of park hours since it opened. Still, the ride's high-profile breakdowns attracted the attention of an Ohio state lawmaker who has proposed a bill that would require amusement-park operators to give real-time notifications if a ride goes out of service.

Building a new roller coaster is costly and complicated, and some of Six Flags' new rides have fallen behind schedule. A new dual-launch coaster called Quantum Accelerator that was supposed to open at Six Flags New England this summer was delayed to next year. A Six Flags spokesman said the company is working with the coaster's manufacturer to modify elements of the ride. "Shifting the ride to next season will allow us to maximize its marketing impact," he said.

Last November, the company announced it would tear down multiple rides — including Kingda Ka, then the world's tallest roller coaster — at Six Flags Great Adventure in New Jersey and open up new ones next year. In late July, Six Flags announced that the replacements for the shuttered Great Adventure attractions won't open until 2027.

Theme-park fan Matthew Kochosky says he's seen Six Flags parks struggle with operations issues in recent years. Ride breakdowns have been more frequent, he says, and employees don't seem to load rides as quickly as they used to. He also felt that the Six Flags parks were not as clean as they used to be. During a recent visit to Kings Dominion, Kochosky and his teenage son opted to leave the park to grab a bite to eat. The in-park options offered, he said, weren't great.

"I feel like there is an issue of quality across the board," said the 50-year-old Kochosky, who lives near Washington, D.C. He boasts of having ridden over 200 different roller coasters across four countries.

Perfect weather for a roller-coaster ride

Six Flags management declared that last month represented a turning point this season. In July, the company started rolling out its 2026 season passes, which grant admission to any of the company's 42 parks, to tap anticipated pent-up demand.

"While we know we have ground to make up from a tough May and June, these results send a clear message: When the gates are open and the product is strong, people will visit," Zimmerman said during the Aug. 6 earnings call.

One recent Saturday, I went to Six Flags Great Adventure in New Jersey. It was sunny with highs in the upper 70s, and a refreshing breeze was blowing through the park. Perfect weather for a roller-coaster ride.

Nearly an hour after the park's opening at 10:30 a.m., the parking lots were nowhere near capacity. The crowds picked up as the day wore on, but the park was hardly jam-packed as might be expected on a summer weekend before schools start.

I waited over 80 minutes to board the park's newest ride, The Flash: Vertical Velocity, a "super boomerang" coaster that starts by launching riders at 70 miles an hour. But the lines elsewhere were short enough that it was possible to ride each of the 11 roller coasters in operation that day at least once without paying for a pass to skip the regular queues.

Plus, there was hardly any wait to get a chicken-tender lunch or an Italian ice.

Days like these have prompted some analysts to look askance at the bad-weather explanations the company has offered. Yes, the weather forced several parks to close fully some days this year. But bad weather has been cited as having an impact on earnings for the company and its premerger predecessors in most quarters since 2022.

Six Flags rival United Parks and Resorts, which operates the SeaWorld and Busch Gardens theme parks, similarly flagged the extreme weather during the most recent quarter. But unlike at Six Flags, attendance across United's parks rose. It was up nearly 1% year-over-year.

"A lot of parks and attractions were projecting to see lower attendance this season," said Josh Liebman, an industry consultant. "But that's certainly not the case all across the board."

Back on the tracks

Six Flags is teeing up some strategies — chiefly selling land and closing some parks — that could generate funds that would help it build more roller coasters and reduce its debt.

About 90% of the company's earnings before taxes and interest comes from its 15 largest parks, said Six Flags Chief Financial Officer Brian Witherow on the company's most recent earnings call.

Zimmerman said the company was exploring "the potential sale of excess land and other noncore assets." Underperforming parks may be on the chopping block.

Six Flags announced this spring that it plans to shut down its Six Flags America park in Maryland and sell the land, and it will also be selling excess land near Kings Dominion. (Another park, California's Great America, was already slated to close at some point in the future under the terms of a 2022 sale of the land it sits on.)

Credit-rating company S&P Global downgraded Six Flags to a BB- rating on Thursday, saying its debt levels were too high relative to earnings and that the recent attendance issues were expected to delay deleveraging efforts. The cut to its credit rating could increase the company's borrowing costs. A company spokesman said Six Flags remained confident it could reduce its debt through "organic growth in the business and the selective divestiture of noncore assets."

Six Flags said it will continue to spruce up the parks. On a recent visit to Six Flags Over Georgia near his Atlanta-area home, Andrew Hyde, co-host of the In the Loop theme-park podcast, noticed updated signage. A funnel-cake eatery that once had a cartoonish sign was now bedecked with an ornate placard featuring stylish lettering.

"This is the best I've seen this park," said Hyde, who has been visiting for over two decades.

Write to Jacob Passy at jacob.passy@wsj.com

data.title: Putin-Trump talks allow to further seek ways for settlement, Interfax cites Kremlin
data.description:
The Kremlin spokesman said that the talks between Russian President Vladimir Putin and U.S. President Donald Trump allowed the countries to continue seeking ways for settlement, the Interfax news agency reported on Saturday.

"The conversation was indeed very positive, and the two presidents spoke about this. This is the very conversation that allows us to confidently move forward together along the path of searching for settlement options," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said.

He did not elaborate which settlement he meant.
